Separate prefill (compute-bound, parallel) and decode (memory-bound, sequential) phases onto distinct GPU pools; eliminate phase interference; goodput optimization; KV cache transfer via NIXL/NVMe-oF/CXL; 2-7x throughput over collocated serving; deployed by Meta, LinkedIn, Mistral; vLLM disagg_prefill feature; cache-aware CPD for 40% higher sustained throughput on long-context

Generate all K draft tokens in a single drafter forward pass (vs sequential EAGLE autoregression); removes autoregressive ceiling; 1.69x over vanilla EAGLE-3 on NVIDIA B200; 4-5x over standard decoding on coding tasks; parallel drafter head trained via teacher forcing; vLLM v0.16+ integration via parallel_drafting=true; pre-trained heads for Llama, Qwen3

Design dynamic context windows: right info, right format, right time; memory types (episodic, semantic, working); retrieval-augmented context; tool results and MCP integration; context compression for long multi-turn; KV cache prefix reuse; smallest high-signal token set maximizes task success; discipline beyond prompt engineering

CNCF Sandbox project donated March 2026 by IBM, Red Hat, Google; disaggregated prefill/decode on separate K8s pods (compute vs memory-bandwidth optimization); hierarchical KV cache offloading across GPU HBM → CPU DRAM → NVMe → distributed storage; cache-aware LoRA routing; scale-to-zero autoscaling; active-active HA; UCCL-based transport resilience; ~3.1k tok/s per B200 decode GPU, 50k output tok/s on 16×16 B200 prefill/decode topology; compatible with any accelerator (NVIDIA/AMD/Google TPU)

Production-ready (v1.0) open-source distributed inference framework; disaggregated prefill/decode across GPU pools via NVIDIA NIXL (Inference Xfer Library) for KV cache transfer; LLM-aware request routing with disaggregated scheduling; 7× throughput on GB200 NVL72 vs collocated serving; dynamic GPU reallocation based on load; multi-tier KV cache (HBM → DRAM → NVMe); compatible with vLLM, SGLang, TensorRT-LLM, PyTorch; deployed by AWS, Azure, GCP, OCI

Meta's production system that treats kernel optimization as a search problem, not one-shot code generation; explores hundreds of alternative kernel implementations per target operation; works across heterogeneous hardware from a single framework: NVIDIA GPUs, AMD GPUs, Meta's custom MTIA silicon, CPUs; uses retrieval augmentation to pull relevant kernel patterns rather than manual prompt engineering; >60% inference throughput improvement over already-optimized baselines (including torch.compile + vendor libraries) on NVIDIA GPUs for the Andromeda Ads model; >25% training throughput improvement on MTIA; up to 17× over raw PyTorch baselines across diverse ops; reduces expert kernel development time from weeks to hours; deployed in production across Meta's training and inference fleet; published at ISCA 2026

items.topic: Ray on Kubernetes for Production ML at Scale (Uber)
items.concepts: Uber migrated all ML projects from YARN-based Peloton to Ray on Kubernetes in 2024; 1.5–4× improvement in distributed training speed; unified stack: Kubernetes + Ray + PyTorch + vLLM; job management platform with SKU-based filtering for heterogeneous GPU hardware (A100, H100, custom SKUs); supports elastic resource sharing and preemption without manual scheduler tuning; users specify job type and resource requirements — system automatically allocates optimal resources based on current cluster load; Ray clusters scoped per job, each node with multiple GPUs; vLLM used for distributed inference scoring in LLM fine-tuning evaluation loops; ElasticDL-style training allows mid-job scaling without checkpoint restarts; two-part migration: (1) open-source tech stack adoption, (2) enhanced Kubernetes resource management with preemption+priority

items.topic: Five Eras of KV Cache: Tiered Offloading & Distributed KV Sharing
items.concepts: KV cache evolution across 5 generations: (1) per-request GPU buffers with PagedAttention (vLLM 2023) — eliminates memory fragmentation; (2) prefix caching — reuse KV of shared system prompts across requests; (3) radix-tree cross-request structural reuse (SGLang) — cache common prefixes across different users; (4) tiered offloading — spill inactive KV blocks GPU HBM→CPU DRAM→NVMe SSD, serving 10x more concurrent users per GPU (vLLM 0.9.0 async offload connector, sub-100ms restoration); (5) distributed multi-node KV sharing for disaggregated serving — LMCache and llm-d filesystem backend let multiple vLLM servers share a common KV prefix cache over shared storage; NVIDIA ICMSP (BlueField-4 DPU off-GPU KV management, CES 2026); CacheFlow 3D-parallel KV scheduling over tokens, layers, and GPUs; 14x TTFT improvement for long-context inputs via prefix restoration; KV quantization (INT8/INT4) reduces offloading bandwidth by 2-4x

resources.title: Modular: The Five Eras of KVCache

items.topic: Google Cloud AI Hypercomputer & TPU 8th Gen Inference Architecture
items.concepts: TPU 8i (inference): 384 MB on-chip SRAM (3x prior gen), 288 GB HBM for massive KV caches, 19.2 Tb/s ICI bandwidth (doubled), dedicated Collectives Acceleration Engine (5x lower AllReduce latency), 80% better perf/$; TPU 8t (training): 121 exaflops per 9,600-chip superpod, 2 PB unified memory, 1M+ chip clusters via Pathways+JAX; Virgo interconnect: 4x bandwidth, 134K TPUs per datacenter, 80K GPUs with A5X (NVIDIA Vera Rubin NVL72); GKE Inference Gateway: ML-driven capacity-aware request routing (70%+ TTFT reduction), integrates with llm-d CNCF; 4x faster node startup + 80% faster pod startup + 5x faster model loading via Model Streamer + Rapid Cache; Google Cloud Managed Lustre at 10 TB/s / 80 PB for checkpoint I/O; designed for agentic-era RL training (high-throughput SRAM) and disaggregated inference (HBM for KV caches)

items.topic: Meta Adaptive Ranking Model (ARM) — Request-Centric LLM-Scale Inference
items.concepts: Solves the inference trilemma: accuracy vs latency vs cost; replaces one-size-fits-all inference with request-centric routing that dynamically selects model complexity based on predicted request value and user intent; lightweight routing model predicts which model tier (small/fast vs large/accurate) maximizes ROI per request; serves LLM-scale recommendation model at sub-500ms P99 on Instagram at global scale; reduces GPU compute cost ~40% vs always-running full-capacity model by routing simpler requests to smaller models; deployed to Instagram Ads Q4 2025 delivering +3% conversions +5% CTR; key insight: not every request needs the biggest model — the intelligence is in the routing, not just the model; enables teams to run LLM-scale models in production without proportionally increasing infrastructure cost

items.topic: Kubernetes Gateway API Inference Extension (GIE) — Model-Aware LLM Traffic Routing
items.concepts: Standard Kubernetes load balancing (round-robin) creates GPU hotspots for LLM serving: one pod with a long-running 40-token/s generation blocks many short requests that could have been served in 50ms on an idle pod; GIE routes at the model level not the URL level — InferencePool CRD groups backend pods serving the same model, EndpointPicker (EPP) plugin selects the least-loaded pod using real-time inference metrics (queue depth, TTFT, tokens-in-flight, GPU utilization); supports LoRA-adapter-aware routing (route requests for fine-tuned variants to pods that have the adapter warm in GPU memory); per-request priority classes allow latency-critical (sync API) and batch (async pipeline) traffic to coexist on the same fleet; v1.4.0 reached GA in 2026 with conformance suite; agentgateway (Rust) is first conformant implementation; adopted by Istio, NGINX Gateway Fabric, Envoy Gateway; KubeCon EU 2026 identified GIE as the emerging standard contract between the K8s gateway layer and inference-aware schedulers (llm-d, KAI Scheduler); reduces P99 TTFT hotspots by 60-70% vs naive round-robin for mixed short/long request traffic

items.topic: NVIDIA Dynamo Agentic Inference — Full-Stack Optimizations for Multi-Turn Agent Workflows
items.concepts: Agent hints extension: structured metadata (priority level, estimated output length, speculative prefill flags) attached per-request, giving Dynamo's router and runtime agent-aware scheduling and KV cache decisions; v1/responses and v1/messages APIs expose typed content blocks (thinking tokens, tool calls, text) as distinct objects — inference engine applies different cache and scheduling policy per block type; 97.2% KV cache hit rate for multi-agent workflows (vs ~40% for stateless LLM serving) by routing agent turns with shared system prompt to nodes with that prefix already warm; multi-turn streaming: reasoning segments, tool-call events, and response metadata stream back as the turn unfolds (not after final text), reducing perceived latency for orchestrators that parse intermediate output; Rust-based parser crates extracted as standalone reusable libraries for harness authors; agent scheduling: long-context reasoning turns routed to high-SRAM nodes, short tool-call dispatch turns routed to low-latency nodes; coding agents (Stripe generating 1,300+ PRs/week, Ramp attributing 30% of merged PRs to agents) as the primary production agentic workload driving these optimizations; Dynamo 1.0 achieves 7× throughput improvement on Blackwell GPUs over baseline collocated serving; published May 2026

resources.title: NVIDIA Technical Blog: Full-Stack Optimizations for Agentic Inference

items.topic: Meta Unified AI Agents for Capacity Efficiency at Hyperscale
items.concepts: Autonomous AI agent platform built on two composable layers: MCP Tools (standardized LLM interfaces for querying profiling data, flamegraphs, code configs, and build systems) and Skills (encoded domain expertise of senior efficiency engineers, reusable across agent workflows); offense + defense strategy — offense agents proactively scan Meta's fleet for compute savings opportunities; defense agents (AI Regression Solver) integrate with Meta's FBDetect CI to auto-detect performance regressions and generate ready-to-review PRs, compressing ~10 hours of manual investigation into ~30 minutes; platform has recovered hundreds of megawatts of power across Meta's AI training fleet (enough to power hundreds of thousands of homes/year); within 1 year the same agent foundation was extended to: conversational efficiency assistants, capacity planning agents, personalized optimization recommendations, guided investigation workflows, and automated validation pipelines; agents operate fully autonomously on production infrastructure changes — the platform IS the Capacity Efficiency Program; the key architectural insight: standardized MCP tool interfaces enable any LLM agent to query the same data that human engineers consult, unlocking automation at scale across all efficiency disciplines; published April 2026

items.topic: Ray Serve 2.55 Online Inference Upgrade — HAProxy Ingress + gRPC Replica Communication
items.concepts: Ray Serve 2.55 introduces two architectural bypasses that eliminate Python-layer bottlenecks: (1) HAProxy integration at ingress — replacing the Python-based HTTP proxy with HAProxy for connection multiplexing, removes the Python GIL from the hot path; (2) Direct gRPC between deployment replicas — bypassing the previous REST-over-Python relay for inter-replica calls. Together these deliver 88% lower P99 latency and 11.1x throughput improvement in unary workloads (490 → 1,573 QPS in recommendation model pipelines), 8.9x in streaming. Both enabled via environment variables in Ray 2.55+; no code changes required for existing Ray Serve deployments.

resources.title: Major upgrades to Ray Serve: Online Inference with 88% lower latency and 11.1x higher throughput

items.topic: Netflix Model Lifecycle Graph — Centralized ML Metadata Service for Discovery and Dependency Tracking
items.concepts: Netflix's Metadata Service (MDS) builds a Model Lifecycle Graph that indexes ML entities (models, features, pipelines, experiments, datasets) and their cross-domain relationships across all of Netflix's ML teams. Architecture: real-time event ingestion → Datomic persistence → background enrichment jobs materialize relationships asynchronously → AIP Portal (unified search + navigation UI). Enables cross-team questions like 'Which experiments use this feature?' or 'Which models share these features?' without manual discovery. Mirrors LinkedIn DataHub's graph-centric approach but tailored for Netflix's multi-team ML ecosystem. Part 1 of a multi-part series on Netflix's 1M req/sec ML serving infrastructure (traffic routing via Switchboard + Envoy in Part 2).

resources.title: Democratizing ML at Netflix: Building the Model Lifecycle Graph
State of Routing in Model Serving (Part 2)

items.topic: vLLM v0.23.0 Multi-Tier KV Cache Offloading + Model Runner V2 + Rust Frontend
items.concepts: Object-store secondary tier for KV cache enables GPU HBM → CPU DRAM → S3/GCS/ABS cascade offloading; per-request offloading policy configurable via on_new_request lifecycle hook for fine-grained GPU memory management under bursty traffic. CUTLASS FP8 padding bypass delivers +20% throughput on FP8 GEMM ops for H100/B200 workloads; MoE-permute buffer pre-allocation yields +9–14% on Mixtral/DeepSeek MoE models. Model Runner V2 now default for Llama and Mistral dense models with FlashInfer samplers and breakable CUDA graphs. Experimental Rust frontend with streaming endpoints bypasses Python GIL on the critical request-handling path. DFlash Causal support with proper lookahead-slot allocation for speculative decoding; DeepSeek-V4 maturity; Transformers v5 compatibility.

items.topic: SGLang v0.5.13 HiCache Hierarchical KV Offload + Speculative Decoding V2 Default
items.concepts: HiCache with UnifiedRadixTree enables automatic hierarchical KV cache offloading for hybrid models (Mamba, SWA) — GPU HBM → CPU DRAM → NVMe SSD; token-count bucket warm-up eliminates cold-start stalls on first serving request after model load. Speculative Decoding V2 (tree drafting with topk > 1) is now the production default across all backends (triton, FA3, MLA, aiter), supporting page_size > 1 and hybrid-linear models; Spec V1 deprecated with EAGLE/MTP unified onto the V2 worker path. DeepSeek V4 context parallelism combined with MTP speculation, flash_mla_sparse_fwd sparse-attention kernel, FP4 indexer support, and waterfill load balancing for DeepEP expert parallelism. SM120 GPU (Blackwell) compatibility added; collectively reduces per-step overhead under high concurrency.

items.topic: Meta SilverTorch: Unified Model-Based Recommendation — Index as Model
items.concepts: SilverTorch reimagines Meta's retrieval ecosystem by making every retrieval component (item index, eligibility filter, scoring layer, user tower) a tensor or operator inside a single PyTorch model. Traditional recommendation pipelines treat index lookup, candidate filtering, and scoring as separate systems with separate deployments, build/deploy cycles, and optimization domains; SilverTorch unifies all of these as nn.Modules in a single PyTorch training and inference graph. Key architectural insight: the boundary between ML engineering and infrastructure engineering dissolves — the whole system reduces to a single PyTorch model, so it automatically benefits from every advance in the PyTorch ecosystem (torch.compile, quantization, hardware-specific kernels, CUDA graph capture) without separate per-component optimization work. Enables direct integration of LLM capabilities inside the retrieval model rather than orchestrating LLMs as an external service — tighter coupling raises the system ceiling for LLM-powered recommendation at production scale. GPU-optimized end-to-end retrieval: the unified model runs entirely on GPU for both training and serving, eliminating the CPU-GPU transfer overhead that constrained index lookup in prior architectures. Democratizes large-scale GPU recommendation infrastructure: any team can train and deploy on standard GPU hardware without maintaining a custom index serving system. Accepted at SIGIR 2026 full paper track; deployed in production at Meta for major recommendation surfaces. Published May 26, 2026.

items.topic: Disaggregated Prefill-Decode Serving (NVIDIA Dynamo / vLLM / SGLang)
items.concepts: Separates the two LLM generation phases — prefill (compute-bound: processes entire prompt in parallel) and decode (memory-bandwidth-bound: generates one token per step) — onto dedicated GPU pools that scale independently. Prefill workers use compute-heavy GPUs (e.g., H100 SXM with high FLOPs); decode workers use memory-bandwidth-optimized GPUs (e.g., H100 PCIe). NVIDIA Dynamo 1.0 (GA March 2026, GTC) sits above vLLM/SGLang/TRT-LLM as an orchestration layer: it routes each request to a prefill worker, then transfers the completed KV-cache tensors to a decode worker via NIXL over NVLink/InfiniBand. Benchmarks on DeepSeek-R1 on Blackwell: up to 7× higher throughput vs monolithic serving. The architecture enables per-phase autoscaling — a KEDA rule on TTFT percentile scales prefill workers while decode workers scale on TBT (time-between-tokens). Production adopters: Meta, LinkedIn, Mistral, Hugging Face running vLLM with disaggregated serving; AWS, GCP, Azure, OCI all announced Dynamo integrations for managed inference (June 2026). Key operational risk: KV-cache transfer adds 5–15ms of cross-node latency; prefill/decode pool size ratio must be tuned per traffic mix (long prompts → more prefill; high output length → more decode); KEDA scale-down of decode workers can deadlock in-flight KV transfers (covered in vLLM Disaggregated Prefill drill).

resources.title: PyTorch Disaggregated Inference at Scale with vLLM

items.topic: Scaling LLM Inference with N-D Parallelism at Meta (Tensor + Context + Expert Parallelism)
items.concepts: Meta's engineering blog (Oct 2025) documents how they scaled LLM inference beyond single-node limits by combining three parallelism dimensions simultaneously: (1) Tensor Parallelism (TP) — splits individual weight matrices across GPUs (2–8 devices), reducing per-device memory at the cost of AllReduce synchronization on every attention/FFN layer; optimal TP degree trades off memory vs NVLink bandwidth saturation; (2) Context Parallelism (CP) — distributes the input sequence across GPUs so each device processes a shard of the KV sequence; enables serving 128K+ token contexts without per-device KV memory explosion; ring-attention pattern required to avoid quadratic memory growth; (3) Expert Parallelism (EP) — for MoE (Mixture of Experts) models like Llama-4, distributes expert layers across separate GPU groups so each token's selected experts may live on different devices; AlltoAll collective required for expert routing, latency-sensitive at scale. Meta moved to a prefill/decode disaggregated architecture alongside N-D parallelism, enabling heterogeneous hardware allocation — prefill uses compute-optimal TP configs while decode uses bandwidth-optimal TP configs; both tiers now served by vLLM with Meta-internal disagg integration. Key operational complexity: N-D parallelism requires careful tuning of TP × CP × EP product against GPU count and NVLink topology; incorrect configs produce silent performance cliffs (not errors); a TP-8 config on H100s connected via PCIe (not NVLink) is 3× slower than TP-8 on NVLink SXM due to bandwidth difference.

resources.title: Meta Engineering: Scaling LLM Inference — Tensor, Context, Expert Parallelism

items.topic: LLM Router / Proxy Architecture (LiteLLM, Portkey, OpenRouter)
items.concepts: Production LLM deployments increasingly use a proxy routing layer between application services and the model backends. Architecture: unified API endpoint (OpenAI-compatible /v1/chat/completions) → router → backend pool (self-hosted vLLM/SGLang + commercial API fallbacks). Key capabilities: (1) Model failover with configurable health checks, cooldown timers, and success retry thresholds — misconfigured cooldown_time (too high) causes runaway spend on commercial fallbacks when primary is transiently unhealthy; (2) Cost tracking per model/team/department with real-time budget enforcement and hard spending caps per time window; (3) Semantic caching — deduplicate semantically similar prompts (e.g., LiteLLM Redis cache, GPTCache) to cut token spend by 20–60% on repetitive workloads; (4) Load balancing strategies: round-robin, latency-based-routing (route to lowest P50 backend), least-busy (route to lowest queue depth); (5) A/B model testing: split traffic by % across model versions for quality comparison with automatic fallback on increased error rates. Operational pitfalls: router itself becomes a critical dependency — must be HA with ≥2 replicas and a circuit breaker for its own health endpoints; per-request health check adds 2–10ms latency overhead (prefer background health polling for latency-sensitive paths); careless failover configuration causes $10K+/hr surprise costs when commercial fallbacks activate under load.
